Q: Made Quant progress after slowing down and focusing on the concept being tested instead of rushing through problems.
In the final push, practiced Quant through OG, GMAT Club filtered sets, Jamboree, Manhattan Prep, and Kaplan sources.
Used GMAT Club error logs and explanations to identify recurring Quant patterns.
Sharpened fundamentals rather than simply increasing question volume.
V: Built Verbal confidence through daily sets of 10-12 questions.
Read explanations carefully, especially for Sentence Correction and Critical Reasoning, to identify patterns.
Used official guide questions from multiple years as the main Verbal resource in the final stretch.
Focused on understanding why answers were wrong rather than moving on after each miss.
overall: Improved from 450 to 655 over a two-year journey with several restarts and score setbacks.
Learned that the fit of a teacher or course matters and that poor guidance can slow motivation.
Created a realistic work-compatible plan of weekday and weekend study after earlier inconsistent bursts failed.
After a painful official score drop, took time to analyze what happened instead of immediately repeating the same approach.
Used timed sections more than full mocks in the final three months to keep intensity without burning out.

Q: Skimmed the official Quant review, then completed OG easy, medium, and hard questions untimed.
For uncertain or slow Quant questions, checked official and GMAT Club solutions and wrote down new formulas.
Used GMAT Club filters by topic, difficulty, and source to drill 5-10 timed questions per topic near the target score level.
Reviewed bookmarked GMAT Club explanations and Forum Quiz misses before the exam.
After a two-month break, refreshed Quant with newer OG books and GMAT Club corrections.
V: Used GMAT Ninja's free videos for Verbal foundation.
Completed OG Verbal questions untimed before moving to timed work.
Recommended sticking to official-source questions on GMAT Club for Verbal because non-official questions felt less reliable.
Returned to OG Verbal and DI work if mocks remained below target after several attempts.
DI: Skimmed the official DI review, then completed DI questions in the official online question bank untimed.
Recommended official-source DI practice when using GMAT Club filters.
Used timed redos of official online question bank items to test whether formulas and approaches had been retained.
overall: Moved from very low initial accuracy to 655, then 695 on a second attempt.
Built the plan around untimed mastery first, then timed topic drills and mocks.
Used mocks after completing corrections and foundational work rather than as the first learning tool.
If a mock was more than 30 points below target, returned to corrections and topic-specific drilling before the next mock.
Kept a separate list of formulas, corrections, and bookmarked explanations for pre-exam review.

Q: Relied partly on DI prep to maintain Quant fundamentals, then targeted Numbers and Inequalities when mocks exposed gaps.
Practiced 700-800-level Quant questions.
Explained Quant questions to a study buddy to stay actively connected to the material.
Used the final week to fix weaker Quant areas rather than blindly taking more full tests.
V: Practiced a couple of RC passages daily during the first month of Focus prep.
Reviewed the PowerScore CR Bible, especially reasoning and flaw chapters.
For RC, focused on contrast words and the reasoning behind eliminating incorrect choices.
Used CR inference skill to support RC performance.
Discussed RC and CR eliminations with a study buddy for additional reasoning practice.
DI: Used GMAT Ninja's DI series, DI Butler questions on GMAT Club, and a TTP trial overview to learn the new section.
Shifted about 80% of effort to DI after a mock with missed DI questions and a large penalty.
Used GMAT Club Pro tests and sectionals to improve DI pacing.
Sometimes skipped time-consuming MSR questions during tests and returned later to edit answers.
Practiced many DI problems to build familiarity with question types and timing.
overall: Moved from a classic-GMAT 680 plateau to a 695 Focus score after a break and renewed prep.
Used timed practice and error logs as central tools.
Experimented with section order but returned to the usual V-Q-DI sequence near test day.
The day before the exam, practiced only during the scheduled exam time frame and reviewed inspiring debriefs to stay calm.
Used a study buddy for motivation and mutual explanation.

Q: Maintained a strong Quant base through weekly sectionals, then diagnosed mock fluctuations as behavioral rather than conceptual.
Slowed down after realizing rushed solving caused missed constraints and process lapses.
Used extra time on the online official exam to revisit and correct a tricky first Quant question.
Followed Q-V-DI order and relied on Quant as the first section.
V: Started Verbal from a low baseline and rebuilt through Master Comprehension.
Used Master Comprehension to break down arguments for both CR and RC.
Replaced intuition in CR with pre-thinking, improving timing and accuracy.
Built RC efficiency by breaking down passages and understanding structure before moving into quizzes.
DI: Treated DI as a combination of Verbal and Quant skills and completed GITA, TPA, DS, and MSR coursework.
Used cementing practice after each DI subsection before moving into sectional readiness quizzes.
Targeted roughly 90% accuracy on medium DI questions and 65% on hard questions.
Used an error log to identify mistake patterns and improve DI timing.
Took relaxed-mode quizzes before returning to standard timing when DI and Verbal pacing were unstable.
overall: Improved from 615 to 695 in three months using a seven-milestone structured plan.
Used clear target metrics for Q, V, DI, and overall score.
Relied on mentor feedback to stay on track and adjust behaviors.
Used Sigma-X and official mocks to gauge readiness because their feel was close to the actual exam.
Chose the online exam to avoid travel friction and preserve focus.

Q: Used sectionals to build the stamina of solving a full 45-minute section rather than isolated questions.
Reached Quant after Verbal on test day and recovered rhythm enough to bookmark, review, and correct at least one answer.
Used structured foundation-building and cementing across sections rather than consuming official books without direction.
V: Chose V-Q-DI order but later recognized that starting with the anxiety-heavy Verbal section may have hurt performance.
Used mentor conversations in the final 10 days to manage mindset and section navigation.
Treated Verbal performance as partly a test-day settling issue, not only a knowledge issue.
DI: Improved DI by 17 points after adopting an 'own the dataset' approach: understand the data before reading the specific question.
For MSR, first inspected the tabs and their relationships to the headings before moving into questions.
Studied DI question types systematically, including DS, TPA, and MSR.
Used cementing quizzes first in relaxed timing to apply methods correctly, then moved to standard timing.
Took 4-5 DI sectional mocks to learn which questions to tackle first, which needed more time, and when to let go.
overall: Improved from a 545 cold mock to 685 over six months of structured preparation.
Moved away from unstructured self-study after realizing that consuming official books was not producing progress.
Used a personalized study plan to map section timing, cementing quizzes, sectionals, and pre-test prep.
Stayed several days ahead of schedule when possible to absorb work disruptions.
Viewed the final phase as mindset management and execution rather than simply learning more content.

Q: Used a short TTP trial period for Quant brush-up, focusing on chapter tests and skipping lower-level material.
Took GMAT Club mocks daily and practiced the topics missed in each mock that same day.
Saved interesting missed questions as an error log.
Improved speed and reduced silly mistakes by treating easy-looking Quant questions as potential traps.
V: Completed the GMAT Ninja playlist and practiced Verbal questions from GMAT Club.
Focused mainly on CR structure rather than heavy Verbal rebuilding because Q and V were already above expectations.
DI: Learned DI mostly through mocks because dedicated material felt limited.
Recognized DS as a likely improvement area for a future retake.
Changed section order to DI-Q-V so DI could be attempted with a fresh mind.
Chose to skip MSR entirely on the official test because it had been a persistent weakness and time sink.
Planned to focus any retake effort mainly on DI.
overall: Moved from inconsistent prior prep to one focused month of work-from-home preparation.
Used GMAT Club mocks, official mocks, official guides, and short free trials rather than a long course.
Treated the first attempt as a best-effort run with low expectations because mocks had stayed below the final score.
Accepted some strategic risk on test day, especially in section order and MSR skipping.
